faq.q: How do I install Ollama on my computer?
faq.a: Ollama supports macOS, Windows, and Linux. On Mac, download from ollama.com and drag to Applications. On Linux, run `curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh`. On Windows, download and run the installer. The entire process takes under 2 minutes.

faq.q: Is Ollama free to use?
faq.a: Yes, Ollama is 100% free and open source. It runs AI models locally on your computer with no API costs, no subscriptions, and no usage limits.

faq.q: What are the system requirements for running local AI with Ollama?
faq.a: You need at least 8GB RAM for small models (3B parameters). For better performance, 16GB+ RAM is recommended. A modern CPU is sufficient — GPU is optional but speeds up inference significantly.

faq.q: How do I connect Ollama to OpenClaw?
faq.a: After installing Ollama and pulling a model, OpenClaw automatically detects Ollama at http://localhost:11434. Just start both applications and they connect seamlessly.

faq.q: What are Ollama cloud models?
faq.a: Ollama now offers cloud models (marked with :cloud suffix) that run on Ollama's servers. You get free quota to try them — no GPU, no hardware needed. Great for getting started before investing in local hardware.

faq.q: How do I install OpenClaw with Ollama?
faq.a: Just run `ollama launch openclaw` in your terminal. Ollama will automatically download, install, configure, and start OpenClaw for you. It's the fastest way to get an AI agent running.

faq.q: What is the difference between Ollama free and Pro plans?
faq.a: Free tier includes local model usage (unlimited) plus limited cloud model quota. Pro ($20/month) gives you more cloud model usage for daily work. Max tier is for heavy cloud usage. Local models are always free regardless of plan.

virtualmachine.title: Zero-to-Hero: Run AI in a Virtual Machine in 10 Minutes
virtualmachine.subtitle: No tech experience needed — learn what a VM is, install Linux with one click, and run your own AI inside it

steps.title: What Is a Virtual Machine?
steps.content: A virtual machine (VM) is like a computer inside your computer. It lets you run a completely separate operating system — like Linux — inside a window on your Mac, without affecting anything on your main system. Think of it as a sandbox: you can experiment freely, and if anything goes wrong, just delete the VM and start over.

subsections.label: Why use a VM for AI?
subsections.text: Running AI models in a VM keeps your main system clean. You get a dedicated Linux environment optimized for AI tools like Ollama, without worrying about messing up your Mac's settings. It's also great for learning Linux basics in a safe environment.

subsections.label: UTM vs Virtual Buddy
subsections.text: Both are Mac apps for running VMs. Virtual Buddy is simpler — one-click Linux install, perfect for beginners. UTM is free and more powerful, with support for many operating systems. We'll cover both so you can choose.

steps.expectedResult: You understand what a VM is and why it's useful for running AI. You're ready to pick your tool.
tips: VMs are completely isolated — nothing you do inside affects your Mac
You can run multiple VMs at once if you have enough RAM
Apple Silicon Macs (M1/M2/M3/M4) run Linux VMs extremely fast

items.q: Will a VM slow down my Mac?
items.a: Only slightly while the VM is running. Close the VM and your Mac returns to full speed. With 16GB+ RAM, you won't notice any slowdown.

items.q: Is this the same as Docker?
items.a: Similar idea, but VMs run a full operating system with its own desktop. Docker runs lightweight containers without a full OS. For beginners, VMs are easier to understand.

steps.title: Install UTM or Virtual Buddy
steps.content: Choose one: Virtual Buddy for the easiest experience (paid, from Mac App Store), or UTM for a free, full-featured option. Both work great on Apple Silicon Macs.
steps.expectedResult: You have UTM or Virtual Buddy installed and ready to create your first VM.
tips: Virtual Buddy: $10 on Mac App Store, one-click Linux setup — perfect for beginners
UTM: 100% free and open source, more configuration options
Both support Apple Silicon natively for best performance

items.a: Go to System Settings → Privacy & Security, click "Open Anyway". This is macOS's standard security for apps downloaded outside the App Store.
Virtual Buddy needs macOS Ventura or later. If you're on an older version, use UTM instead — it supports macOS 11+.
If you want zero hassle and don't mind paying $10, go with Virtual Buddy. If you prefer free software or want more control, choose UTM.

commands: # Virtual Buddy (recommended for beginners, one-click Linux)
# Download Virtual Buddy from Mac App Store
# Download UTM from the official website

steps.title: Create a Linux VM
steps.content: Now let's create your Linux virtual machine. Virtual Buddy makes this a one-click process. With UTM, you'll download an Ubuntu image and follow a short setup wizard.

subsections.label: Why Ubuntu?
subsections.text: Ubuntu is the most beginner-friendly Linux distribution. It has a graphical desktop, tons of online help, and excellent support for AI tools. Most AI tutorials and documentation assume Ubuntu.

steps.expectedResult: A Linux (Ubuntu) VM is running in a window on your Mac. You can see the Ubuntu desktop or terminal.
tips: Allocate at least 4GB RAM and 2 CPU cores to the VM for smooth AI performance
20GB disk space is enough to start; you can expand later
The VM downloads Ubuntu automatically — no need to find ISO files yourself with Virtual Buddy

items.a: Make sure you allocated enough RAM (4GB minimum). Close other heavy apps on your Mac. Also ensure you're using the ARM64 version of Ubuntu, not x86.

items.q: Screen is black after creating VM
items.a: Wait 1-2 minutes — the first boot takes time to initialize. If still black, try restarting the VM.

items.q: Where to download Ubuntu ISO for UTM?
items.a: Go to ubuntu.com/download/server and download the ARM64 version for Apple Silicon Macs.

multiagent.title: Multi-Agent Deployment — Advanced OpenClaw Setup

steps.title: Why Multi-Agent?
steps.content: A single OpenClaw agent is great for individual tasks, but real-world workflows often need specialization. Multi-agent deployment lets you run separate agents for different roles — a coder, a researcher, a writer — and have them collaborate through shared context and message routing.
steps.expectedResult: You understand when multi-agent setups are better than a single agent.
tips: Multi-agent shines when tasks need different expertise — e.g. coding + documentation
Each agent can use a different model (fast model for triage, powerful model for coding)
Agents can run on different machines across your network
Start simple with 2 agents before scaling up

items.a: No! You can run multiple agents on one machine. Each agent is a separate OpenClaw process with its own configuration.

items.q: Is this the same as Paperclip?
items.a: Paperclip is the management layer that coordinates agents. OpenClaw is the runtime engine for each individual agent. You can use multi-agent with or without Paperclip.

commands: # Agent 1: Researcher — uses Perplexity + web search

steps.title: Set Up Your First Agent Pair

Give each agent a clear, focused system prompt — specialization is key
The planner can use a cheaper/faster model since it's only routing tasks
The worker should use a more capable model for actual implementation

items.q: Port conflict when starting second agent
items.a: Each OpenClaw instance needs its own port. Use --port 3001 for the second agent, --port 3002 for the third, etc.
Agents communicate through the filesystem or an API bridge, not directly. Make sure they share a common workspace directory.

"systemPrompt": "You are a task planner. Break down user requests into clear, actionable subtasks. Output a numbered task list.",
"systemPrompt": "You are a task executor. Complete the assigned task precisely and report the result.",

steps.title: Configure Agent Collaboration
steps.content: Now connect your agents so they can collaborate. The planner breaks tasks down and writes them to a shared directory. The worker picks up tasks and executes them. This simple file-based approach works well for local setups.
steps.expectedResult: Agents can pass tasks to each other through the shared workspace.
tips: File-based handoff is the simplest pattern — great for getting started
For production, consider using a message queue (Redis, RabbitMQ) instead
Add a watcher script to auto-assign tasks to the worker agent
Log all agent interactions for debugging and improvement

items.a: Check that the shared directory path is correct in both configs. Also verify the watcher script is running: ps aux | grep openclaw-watcher
Add a lock file mechanism or use atomic file operations. The watcher should rename tasks from .pending to .processing to prevent double-pickup.

steps.content: Scale beyond two agents by adding specialized roles. A reviewer agent can check the worker's output, a researcher agent can gather information, and a summarizer can compile results. Each role gets a focused system prompt and optimal model.
steps.expectedResult: You have a multi-agent team with specialized roles running locally.
tips: Don't over-specialize — 3-4 agents is usually the sweet spot
Use Ollama's lighter models (phi3, gemma2) for simple routing tasks
Reserve powerful models (llama3.1:70b, Claude) for complex reasoning
Monitor memory usage — each agent consumes RAM for its loaded model

items.a: Use smaller models for simple agents. Ollama shares model weights between instances, so multiple agents using the same model are efficient. Or offload some agents to Zeabur cloud.
Set temperature to 0 for deterministic outputs. Also ensure system prompts are specific — vague prompts lead to inconsistent behavior.

faq.q: What is multi-agent AI orchestration?
faq.a: Multi-agent orchestration coordinates multiple AI agents to work together on complex tasks. Each agent specializes in different capabilities, and they communicate to solve problems no single agent could handle alone.

faq.q: How do I set up multiple AI agents?
faq.a: Start with a primary agent (like Ollama), then add specialized agents for tasks like web search, code generation, or data analysis. Use an orchestration framework to manage communication between them.

hardware.title: How to Choose AI Hardware — Complete Buying Guide
hardware.subtitle: Find the right hardware for running local AI models. From budget-friendly Raspberry Pi to powerful mini PCs — we compare specs, price, and use cases.

steps.title: Understand Your AI Hardware Needs
steps.content: Before buying hardware, figure out what you want to run. Small models (1-3B parameters) work on almost anything. Medium models (7-13B) need 16GB+ RAM. Large models (30B+) need 32GB+ RAM and a beefy CPU or GPU. Your use case determines your budget.
tips: Running Ollama with Llama 3.2 (3B)? Even a Raspberry Pi 5 can handle it
Want to run Llama 3.1 (8B) smoothly? Get at least 16GB RAM
Planning an always-on AI server? Prioritize power efficiency and passive cooling
Budget under $100? Start with Raspberry Pi 5. Budget $400-500? Go with a mini PC

items.q: Do I need a GPU for local AI?
items.a: Not necessarily. Ollama runs models on CPU just fine. A GPU speeds up inference but is not required. Integrated GPUs (like AMD Radeon 780M) help a lot.

items.q: Can I use my existing Mac or PC?
items.a: Absolutely! You can run Ollama on any Mac (M1+), Windows, or Linux machine. Dedicated hardware is only needed if you want an always-on AI server.

**$400-450 — Beelink SER5 MAX — $439**

**$500+ — GEEKOM A8 Mini PC — $529+**
Our top pick. AMD Ryzen 7 8745HS, 32GB DDR5, Radeon 780M GPU. Handles 13B models and runs multiple agents simultaneously. Upgradeable to 64GB RAM.

items.a: Make sure you're using 64-bit Raspberry Pi OS. Run 'uname -m' — it should show 'aarch64'. If it shows 'armv7l', you need to reinstall with the 64-bit image.

always-on-mac.title: Make Your Mac Always-On — AI Agent Setup Guide
always-on-mac.subtitle: Keep your Mac Mini or MacBook running 24/7 for OpenClaw and AI agents. Learn power settings, HDMI dummy plug setup, and TCP keepalive configuration.

steps.title: Why Always-On Matters for AI Agents
steps.titleEn: Why Always-On Matters for AI Agents
steps.content:
AI agents like OpenClaw need a persistent connection to work. When your Mac sleeps, it disconnects TCP connections — meaning your agents stop responding to messages, lose context, and miss events.

The fix depends on your hardware:

**MacBook users:** Adjust power settings and keep the lid open. You can dim the screen to save energy.

**Mac Mini users:** You face an extra challenge. When your external monitor turns off (or is disconnected), macOS thinks there is no display. This breaks VM screen capture, GPU acceleration, and remote desktop. The solution? An HDMI Dummy Plug — a tiny device that tricks macOS into thinking a monitor is always connected.
tips: MacBook: keep the lid open, dim the screen to minimum
Mac Mini: plug in an HDMI Dummy Plug before configuring power settings
Both: keep your Mac connected to power at all times

items.a: No. Preventing sleep is safe. Macs are designed for extended operation.

items.q: Will this increase my electricity bill?
items.a: Barely. A Mac Mini uses ~5-7W at idle. That is about $5-10/year in electricity.

steps.title: Configure Power Settings
steps.content:
Open Terminal and run these commands to prevent your Mac from sleeping. This works on both MacBook and Mac Mini.

For AC power (recommended for always-on setups):
steps.expectedResult: After running these commands, your Mac will stay awake on AC power. The caffeinate process runs in the background to prevent system sleep. TCP keepalive should show value 1.
tips: The -c flag targets AC power settings only — your Mac still sleeps normally on battery
caffeinate -s prevents system sleep while on AC power
TCP keepalive maintains network connections during low-power states
To verify all settings: run pmset -g custom

items.a: Make sure to use sudo before the pmset command. You will need to enter your Mac password.

items.q: Mac still sleeps after settings change
items.a: Check if caffeinate is running: pgrep caffeinate. If not, re-run the caffeinate command.

steps.title: Mac Mini: Set Up HDMI Dummy Plug
steps.titleEn: Mac Mini: Set Up HDMI Dummy Plug
steps.content:
**This step is for Mac Mini users only.** MacBook users can skip to Step 4.

When a Mac Mini has no display connected, macOS disables GPU-accelerated rendering. This means:
- VMs cannot capture the screen
- Remote desktop shows a blank/low-res image
- AI agents that need screenshots will not work

**The fix: EVanlak 8K HDMI Dummy Plug**

This super tiny plug (world's smallest!) goes into your Mac Mini's HDMI port. macOS instantly thinks a monitor is connected, restoring full GPU acceleration.

**Setup:**
1. Power off your Mac Mini (optional but recommended)
2. Plug the EVanlak HDMI Dummy Plug into any HDMI port
3. Power on — macOS will detect a display immediately
4. Verify: System Settings > Displays should show the dummy display
steps.expectedResult: You should see a display listed (even though no physical monitor is connected). This confirms the dummy plug is working and macOS has full GPU acceleration enabled.
tips: The EVanlak plug supports 8K@30Hz / 4K@120Hz — more than enough for headless operation
You can still use Screen Sharing or VNC to access your Mac Mini remotely
If you use multiple displays, the dummy plug takes one HDMI port — plan accordingly
Buy on Amazon: EVanlak 8K HDMI Dummy Plug (ASIN: B0CL21JLB1)

items.q: Displays does not show the dummy plug
items.a: Try a different HDMI port. If your Mac Mini has USB-C/Thunderbolt, the HDMI dummy plug must go in the HDMI port specifically.

items.q: Remote desktop still shows low resolution
items.a: After plugging in, go to System Settings > Displays and set the resolution to your preferred setting (e.g. 4K).

steps.title: Configure macOS System Settings
steps.content:
In addition to Terminal commands, configure these macOS System Settings for a fully always-on setup:

**Lock Screen:**
- Turn off display when inactive: Never (for both battery and power adapter if on MacBook)

**Screen Saver:**
- Start screen saver: Never

**Energy Saver (System Settings > Battery > Options):**
- Wake for network access: Always
- Optimize video streaming while on battery: Off

**MacBook-specific:**
- You can dim brightness to the lowest setting to save energy
- **Do NOT close the lid** — this forces hardware sleep regardless of pmset settings
- If you must close the lid, use clamshell mode with external display + power adapter
steps.expectedResult: The output should show: sleep 0, disksleep 0, networkoversleep 1, tcpkeepalive 1. This confirms your Mac is fully configured for always-on operation.
tips: MacBook users: keep the lid open and dim the screen instead of closing it
Enable Remote Management in System Settings > General > Sharing for remote access
Consider enabling Screen Sharing for remote GUI access
Set up SSH for terminal access: System Settings > General > Sharing > Remote Login

items.q: My MacBook still sleeps when I close the lid
items.a: Closing the lid triggers hardware sleep — no software setting can override this. Keep the lid open or use clamshell mode with an external display and power adapter.

items.q: How do I undo all of this?
items.a: Run: sudo pmset -c sleep 1 disksleep 10 networkoversleep 0 && pkill caffeinate

steps.title: Verify & Next Steps
steps.content:
Your Mac is now configured for 24/7 always-on operation. Here is how to verify everything is working:

1. Leave your Mac running for 30+ minutes — it should not sleep
2. Check agents are connected: your OpenClaw gateway should maintain its TCP connection
3. For Mac Mini: verify the dummy display is still detected

You are now ready to run AI agents around the clock!

faq.a: Negligible — it draws less than 0.1W from the HDMI port. It is a passive device.

faq.q: Can I use the Mac Mini without any monitor at all?

faq.q: Will always-on reduce my Mac's lifespan?
faq.a: Modern Macs are designed for continuous operation. Apple's own data centers run Mac hardware 24/7. SSDs have very long lifespans, and Apple Silicon is extremely power efficient.

omlx.title: oMLX — 10x Faster AI on Your Mac
omlx.subtitle: Replace LM Studio with oMLX for blazing-fast OpenClaw agent performance on Apple Silicon. Free, open-source, 10 minutes to set up.

steps.title: What is MLX? (Mac Only 🍎)
steps.content:
## Understanding MLX and Apple Silicon

MLX is Apple's open-source machine learning framework designed specifically for Apple Silicon chips. Unlike PyTorch or JAX, MLX leverages Apple's **unified memory architecture** where CPU and GPU share the same memory pool.

### Why This Matters for OpenClaw

OpenClaw agents make **dozens of tool calls per task**. Each call requires re-processing the system prompt (often 2000+ tokens). Without prefix caching:
- **LM Studio**: 20-30 seconds wait EVERY tool call
- **oMLX**: Nearly instant responses (shared prefix cache)

A YouTube creator found: *"Base Mac Mini 16GB went from 5 minutes to answer 3 questions → nearly instant with oMLX"*

### System Requirements

⚠️ **Mac with Apple Silicon ONLY** (M1/M2/M3/M4)
- macOS 15.0+ (Sequoia)
- Python 3.10+
- **16GB RAM minimum** (32GB+ recommended)
- **Intel Macs are NOT supported**

### How MLX Works

Apple Silicon's unified memory means:
- CPU and GPU share same RAM
- No memory copying between processors
- Efficient model loading and inference
- Perfect for LLM workloads
tips: Check your chip: Apple menu > About This Mac → look for 'M1', 'M2', 'M3', or 'M4'
16GB RAM handles most models; 32GB+ for large models and multiple agents
MLX models are different from regular PyTorch models (but oMLX handles conversion automatically)

items.q: I have an Intel Mac — can I use oMLX?
items.a: No. oMLX requires Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) and won't run on Intel Macs. Consider using Ollama instead for Intel Macs.

items.q: My Mac has 8GB RAM — will it work?
items.a: Not recommended. 8GB systems struggle with modern LLMs. Consider cloud models via Ollama or upgrading to 16GB+ RAM.

steps.title: Install oMLX
steps.content:
## Two Installation Methods

Choose the method that suits your preference:

### Method 1: DMG Download (Recommended)

1. Visit [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/jundot/omlx/releases)
2. Download the latest `.dmg` file
3. Drag oMLX.app to Applications folder
4. Open oMLX — it will appear in your menu bar

**Benefits**: Auto-update, GUI interface, menu bar convenience

### Method 2: Homebrew Install

For developers who prefer command line:

```bash
# Add the oMLX tap
brew tap jundot/omlx https://github.com/jundot/omlx

# Install oMLX
brew install omlx

# Start oMLX service
brew services start omlx
```

**Benefits**: Command-line control, easy updates with `brew upgrade`

## Verify Installation

After installation, oMLX should start automatically:

```bash
# Check if oMLX is running
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models

# Should return JSON with models list
```

You'll see the oMLX icon in your menu bar (DMG install) or service running in background (Homebrew).
tips: DMG version includes built-in auto-update mechanism
Homebrew version can run as background service automatically
oMLX uses port 8000 by default (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)

items.a: Go to System Settings → Privacy & Security, scroll down and click "Open Anyway" next to the oMLX warning.
Another service is using port 8000. Kill it with `lsof -ti:8000 | xargs kill` or configure oMLX to use different port.
Make sure you have Command Line Tools installed: `xcode-select --install`. Then retry the installation.

steps.title: Download Your First Model
steps.content:
## Launch oMLX and Download Models

1. **Open oMLX** (from Applications or menu bar)
2. **Set Model Directory**: Choose where to store models (default: `~/models`)
3. **Use Built-in Downloader**: oMLX includes a model browser/downloader

## Recommended Models by RAM

### 16GB RAM Systems
| Model | Size | Speed | Best For |
|-------|------|-------|----------|
| Qwen3-4B-MLX-4bit | ~3GB | Fast | Daily tasks, coding |
| glm-4.7-flash-4bit | ~3GB | Fast | Reasoning, analysis |

### 32GB RAM Systems
| Model | Size | Speed | Best For |
|-------|------|-------|----------|
| Qwen3-30B-A3B-4bit | ~18GB | Medium | Advanced reasoning |
| Step-3.5-Flash-8bit | ~14GB | Medium | Code generation |

### 64GB+ RAM Systems
| Model | Size | Speed | Best For |
|-------|------|-------|----------|
| Qwen3.5-32B-4bit | ~20GB | Medium | Production tasks |
| DeepSeek-R1-Distill-70B | ~40GB | Slower | Maximum capability |

## Start the Server

Once you've downloaded a model:

```bash
# Start oMLX with your model directory
omlx serve --model-dir ~/models

# Server will start on http://localhost:8000
# Admin panel available at http://localhost:8000/admin
```

## Verify Model Loading

```bash
# List available models
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models

# Test chat completion
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "your-model-name",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}],
    "max_tokens": 100
  }'
```
tips: Start with smaller models to test everything works before downloading large ones
Model download requires internet, but once downloaded they run completely offline
oMLX automatically converts models to MLX format on first load

items.a: Large models can take time. Ensure stable internet connection. Consider starting with smaller 4GB models first.
Check RAM usage — models need to fit in memory. Try a smaller quantized version (4bit instead of 8bit).
Check your model directory setting in oMLX. Default is ~/models. Models are stored in subdirectories by name.

steps.title: Connect to OpenClaw 🦞
steps.content:
## Configure OpenClaw to Use oMLX

oMLX provides an OpenAI-compatible API, so OpenClaw connects seamlessly.

### Method 1: OpenClaw Config Command

```bash
# Edit OpenClaw configuration
openclaw config edit

# Add oMLX provider to your config
```

### Method 2: Manual JSON Edit

Add this to your OpenClaw configuration:

```json
{
  "providers": {
    "omlx": {
      "type": "openai",
      "baseURL": "http://localhost:8000/v1",
      "apiKey": "omlx-local",
      "models": ["*"]
    }
  },
  "defaultModel": "omlx/your-model-name"
}
```

### Test the Connection

```bash
# Start OpenClaw with oMLX
openclaw chat

# In the chat, try a simple command:
# "What's 2+2?" 

# Should respond instantly using your local model
```

## Expected Results

✅ **Before oMLX**: OpenClaw tool calls take 20-30 seconds each  
✅ **After oMLX**: Tool calls respond in 1-2 seconds  
✅ **System prompt caching**: Shared context across multiple agents  
✅ **Multiple agents**: Run concurrent OpenClaw instances  

## Why This Works

OpenClaw agents make frequent tool calls, each requiring system prompt reprocessing. oMLX's **prefix caching** means:
- First tool call: Normal speed
- Subsequent calls: Nearly instant (cached prefix)
- Multiple agents: Share same cached system prompt
tips: Keep oMLX running in the background for best performance
Set oMLX as your default provider to use it automatically
You can still use cloud providers for specific tasks while having oMLX as default

items.a: Verify oMLX is running: `curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models`. Check firewall isn't blocking port 8000.
Check model name exactly matches what's shown in `curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models`. Names are case-sensitive.
First few requests may be slower while building cache. Try the same prompt multiple times — subsequent ones should be much faster.

steps.title: Optimize: Cache & Concurrency
steps.content:
## The Secret Sauce: Advanced Caching

This is where oMLX shines for OpenClaw agents.

### Tiered KV Caching

**Hot Cache (RAM)**:
- Active conversations and system prompts
- Ultra-fast access (microseconds)
- Set to ~80% of available RAM

**Cold Cache (SSD)**:
- Background contexts and longer conversations
- Survives restarts and system reboots
- Set to available SSD space (50-200GB+)

### Admin Panel Configuration

Visit **http://localhost:8000/admin** for advanced settings:

1. **Cache Settings**:
   - Hot cache: 80% of RAM (e.g., 12GB on 16GB system)
   - Cold cache: 100GB+ on SSD
   
2. **Model Management**:
   - Pin frequently-used models to stay in hot cache
   - Set priority levels for different models
   
3. **Concurrency Settings**:
   - Concurrent requests: Start with 2-4
   - Batch size: Adjust based on model size

### Concurrent Agent Setup

```bash
# Terminal 1: Start first OpenClaw agent
openclaw chat --session=agent1

# Terminal 2: Start second OpenClaw agent  
openclaw chat --session=agent2

# Both agents share the same cached system prompt!
```

### Performance Tuning

```bash
# Check current cache usage
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/cache/stats

# Benchmark your setup
omlx benchmark --model your-model-name

# Monitor performance
top -p $(pgrep omlx)
```

## Expected Performance Gains

| Scenario | Before oMLX | With oMLX |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| Single tool call | 20-30 sec | 1-2 sec |
| 10 tool calls | 5+ minutes | 30 seconds |
| Multiple agents | Multiplied wait | Parallel execution |
| Context switching | Full reload | Instant cache hit |
tips: Set cold cache to your available SSD space — it's persistent across restarts
Pin your main OpenClaw model to keep it always in hot cache
Start with 2 concurrent agents, increase based on your RAM and model size
Monitor memory usage — if you hit swap, reduce cache sizes or use smaller models

items.a: Increase hot cache size in admin panel. If RAM is limited, use smaller quantized models (4bit instead of 8bit).
Check concurrent request limits in admin panel. Start with 2-4 concurrent requests, adjust based on performance.
Verify write permissions to cache directory. Check disk space — oMLX needs space to write cache files.

faq.q: What's the difference between oMLX and LM Studio?
faq.a: LM Studio is a general LLM interface, while oMLX is optimized specifically for AI agents. Key differences: oMLX has prefix caching (shares system prompts across calls), tiered memory management (hot RAM + cold SSD), and concurrent agent support. For OpenClaw specifically, oMLX is 10x+ faster due to cached system prompt processing.

faq.q: Can I use oMLX on an Intel Mac?
faq.a: No. oMLX requires Apple Silicon (M1, M2, M3, or M4 chips) and macOS 15.0+ (Sequoia). It's built on Apple's MLX framework which only works on Apple Silicon. Intel Mac users should stick with Ollama or cloud-based models.

faq.q: How much RAM do I need for oMLX?
faq.a: Minimum 16GB for small models, 32GB+ recommended for production use. Unlike traditional inference, oMLX's caching means you need RAM for: model weights + KV cache + system memory. Rule of thumb: 2x model size in RAM for optimal caching performance.

faq.q: Can I run multiple OpenClaw agents with oMLX?
faq.a: Yes! This is one of oMLX's key advantages. Multiple agents can share the same cached system prompt, making concurrent execution very efficient. Start with 2-4 concurrent agents and adjust based on your RAM and model size.

faq.q: Does oMLX work with Claude Code and other AI coding agents?
faq.a: Absolutely. oMLX provides an OpenAI-compatible API, so it works with any tool that supports OpenAI API format. This includes Claude Code, Codex, Aider, and dozens of other AI coding tools. The speed improvement is especially noticeable for coding agents that make many tool calls.

localAiPrivacy.title: Why Local AI Is the Privacy Breakthrough of 2026
localAiPrivacy.summary: Cloud AI means your data leaves your device. Local AI changes that equation entirely. Here's why running models on your own hardware matters more than ever.
content: Every time you send a prompt to a cloud AI service, your data travels across the internet to a remote server. That server processes it, stores logs, and may use your inputs for training. For personal conversations, business secrets, or creative work, that's a real concern.

Local AI flips this model. With tools like Ollama, you can run powerful language models — Llama 3, Mistral, CodeLlama — entirely on your own machine. Your prompts never leave your device. There are no API keys, no usage fees, and no data retention policies to worry about.

The performance gap between local and cloud AI has narrowed dramatically. A modern laptop with 16GB RAM can run 7B-parameter models with near-instant responses. For coding assistance, writing, and general Q&A, local models are now genuinely competitive.

Setting up local AI used to require deep technical knowledge. Today, Ollama makes it a single command: `ollama run llama3`. That's it — you're running a state-of-the-art language model locally in under 5 minutes.

At CanFly.ai, we believe everyone deserves access to AI that respects their privacy. That's why our free Ollama tutorial is the entry point to our entire platform. Start local, stay private, and upgrade to cloud deployment only when you're ready.
The future of AI isn't just about capability — it's about control. Local AI gives you both.

deployAiCloud.summary: You've built something amazing locally. Now it's time to share it with the world. Here's how to go from local prototype to cloud-hosted AI agent.
content: Building an AI agent on your laptop is exciting. But when you want to share it with teammates, customers, or the world, you need cloud deployment. The good news: it's never been easier.
The traditional path — setting up servers, configuring Docker, managing SSL certificates — is a barrier that stops most creators. Platforms like Zeabur have eliminated this complexity entirely.
With Zeabur, you connect your GitHub repo and click deploy. Your AI agent gets a public URL, automatic HTTPS, and scales automatically based on demand. The entire process takes about 3 minutes.
The key insight is starting local, then deploying to cloud. Build and test with Ollama on your machine where iteration is free and fast. Once your agent works well, push to cloud for production use.

This local-first, cloud-ready approach is the CanFly philosophy. You maintain full control during development, and you only pay for cloud resources when you actually need them. Use referral code 'OpenClaw' for 10% off your first Zeabur deployment.
The transition from prototype to production should be seamless. With the right tools, it is.

voiceVideoCreation.title: AI Voice and Video: The Next Frontier of Content Creation
voiceVideoCreation.summary: Text-based AI is just the beginning. Voice synthesis and AI video are transforming how creators produce content — and it's more accessible than you think.
content: We've entered an era where AI can speak with human-quality voices and generate professional video content. These aren't gimmicks — they're production-ready tools that creators and businesses are using right now.

Voice synthesis through platforms like ElevenLabs has reached a remarkable level of quality. You can clone voices, create multilingual narrations, and produce audiobooks — all from text input. The applications range from accessibility (making content available to visually impaired users) to education (multilingual course delivery).

AI video generation with HeyGen takes this further. Create talking-head videos, product demos, and educational content without cameras, studios, or actors. Upload a script, choose an avatar or use your own likeness, and generate professional video in minutes.

For AI agent builders, these tools open up entirely new capabilities. Imagine an agent that doesn't just respond in text, but speaks to users in their language, or creates video tutorials on demand. The integration possibilities are endless.

The cost structure has also shifted dramatically. What used to require a production studio and a team of specialists can now be done by a single person with the right AI tools. ElevenLabs starts at $5/month, and HeyGen offers a free tier for getting started.

At CanFly.ai, we see voice and video as the natural next step after text-based AI. Our tutorials guide you from free local AI to cloud deployment to premium capabilities like voice and video. It's the complete journey from zero to a fully-capable AI agent.

The content creation landscape is being reshaped by AI. The creators who embrace these tools early will have a significant advantage. The question isn't whether to adopt AI voice and video — it's when.

localAiJourney.title: Building Your Local AI Agent: From Zero to Llama
localAiJourney.summary: The journey from curiosity to a working local AI agent is shorter than you think. Here's a hands-on guide to going from nothing to a Llama-powered agent on your own machine.
content: There's a moment every AI enthusiast remembers: the first time a language model runs on their own hardware. No cloud. No API keys. No monthly bills. Just you and a model, having a conversation on your machine. That moment is now five minutes away.

The Llama family of models — Meta's open-weight language models — changed the game for local AI. Llama 3 delivers responses that rival cloud services, and it runs comfortably on a laptop with 16GB of RAM. Combined with Ollama, which handles model management and serving, you have a complete local AI stack in a single command.

But running a model is just the beginning. The real magic happens when you turn that model into an agent — a system that doesn't just answer questions, but takes actions. An agent can browse the web, write and execute code, manage files, and interact with APIs. All running locally, all under your control.

The architecture is elegant in its simplicity. Ollama serves the model through a local API. Your agent code connects to that API, sends prompts, and receives responses. Add a tool framework — function calling, shell access, or browser automation — and your agent can interact with the real world.

Start with a simple loop: the agent receives a task, breaks it into steps, executes each step using tools, and reports back. This is the foundation of every AI agent, from simple chatbots to complex automation systems. The difference between a toy and a production agent is the quality of the tools and the robustness of the loop.

Privacy is the hidden superpower of local agents. When your agent processes sensitive data — customer information, proprietary code, personal documents — nothing leaves your machine. This isn't just a nice-to-have; for many businesses and individuals, it's a requirement.

The CanFly approach is to start local and expand outward. Master Ollama and Llama on your own hardware first. Then, when you're ready for more power or need to share your agent with others, deploy to the cloud with a single click. The skills transfer perfectly — the same agent that runs on your laptop can run on a server.

autonomousAgents.title: AI Agents That Work For You: Autonomous Task Automation
autonomousAgents.summary: What if your AI agent could fill out forms, register accounts, and handle routine tasks without your input? Autonomous agents are making this real — here's how it works.
content: The promise of AI agents has always been clear: software that works for you, not just with you. Instead of manually clicking through forms, copying data between apps, and repeating the same workflows daily, an agent handles it all. We're finally at the point where this isn't science fiction — it's practical engineering.

Consider a simple but powerful example: automated account registration. An AI agent can navigate to a website, fill out a signup form, verify an email address, and complete onboarding — all without human intervention. The same pattern applies to booking appointments, submitting applications, or monitoring price changes.

The key technology behind autonomous agents is tool use. Modern language models can decide which tools to invoke — a web browser, an email client, a file system — and chain them together to complete multi-step tasks. The model provides the reasoning; the tools provide the actions.

Building your first autonomous agent doesn't require a massive framework. Start with a local model through Ollama, add a browser automation library like Playwright, and write a simple orchestration script. Your agent can read a webpage, extract relevant information, make decisions, and take actions — all running on your own machine.

The challenges are real but solvable. Websites change layouts, CAPTCHAs block automation, and edge cases abound. The best agents handle these gracefully: they retry with different strategies, fall back to human assistance when stuck, and learn from failures. Robustness comes from iteration, not complexity.

Autonomous agents shine in repetitive, time-consuming workflows. Data entry, form submission, content aggregation, competitive monitoring — these are tasks where humans add little creative value but spend significant time. Delegating them to agents frees you to focus on work that actually requires human judgment.

At CanFly.ai, we believe the future is a personal fleet of AI agents, each specialized for different tasks. Start with one agent, one workflow. Prove the value. Then expand. The tools are ready, the models are capable, and the learning curve has never been gentler.

openSourceSkills.title: Open-Source Agent Skills: Why Community Wins
openSourceSkills.summary: Closed AI platforms lock you in. Open-source agent skills let you customize, share, and build on each other's work. Here's why the community approach is winning.
content: When it comes to AI agent capabilities, there are two paths. Closed platforms offer polished but rigid experiences — you use exactly the skills they've built, in exactly the way they allow. Open-source agent skills offer something fundamentally different: the ability to inspect, modify, share, and compose capabilities without permission.

An agent 'skill' is a packaged capability — web browsing, code execution, image generation, API integration. In the open-source world, these skills are published as reusable modules. Anyone can install them, anyone can improve them, and anyone can create new ones. The result is an ecosystem that evolves faster than any single company could manage.

The practical benefits are immediate. Need your agent to interact with a niche API? Someone in the community has probably built a skill for it. Want to customize how your agent browses the web? Fork the skill, modify the code, and run your version. This isn't theoretical — it's how thousands of developers build agents today.

Quality in open-source comes from transparency. When a skill's source code is visible, bugs get found faster, security issues get patched quicker, and best practices emerge organically. Compare this to closed platforms where a bug might persist for months because users can't see or fix the underlying code.

The composability of open-source skills is where things get really powerful. Combine a web-browsing skill with a data-extraction skill and a notification skill, and you have a monitoring agent. Swap the notification skill for an email-drafting skill, and you have a research assistant. The building blocks snap together in ways their individual creators never imagined.

At CanFly.ai, we champion the open-source approach because it aligns with our core belief: AI should empower individuals, not create dependencies. Every tutorial we offer uses open-source tools. Every agent you build on our platform is yours — no vendor lock-in, no permission required, no monthly fee to keep your skills running.

agentEmail.title: AI Agent Email Showdown: BaseMail vs AgentMail vs NadMail
agentEmail.summary: Your AI agent needs its own email address. We tested three leading agent email platforms head-to-head — here's which one wins for different use cases.
content: When your AI agent needs to send emails, receive confirmations, or manage correspondence autonomously, a regular Gmail account won't cut it. You need an email service designed specifically for agents — one with API-first access, programmable inboxes, and no CAPTCHA walls. In 2026, three platforms are leading this space: BaseMail, AgentMail, and NadMail.

BaseMail takes the minimalist approach. It gives your agent a dedicated email address with a clean REST API. Sending is straightforward: POST your message to the API endpoint with your agent's credentials. Receiving works via webhooks — BaseMail pushes incoming emails to your agent's endpoint in real-time. The free tier includes 100 emails per day, which is plenty for most personal agent setups. The catch? Limited attachment support and no built-in threading.

AgentMail is the power user's choice. It offers programmable inboxes where you can define rules — auto-forward certain senders, filter by subject patterns, or trigger agent actions based on email content. The API supports full MIME parsing, rich HTML emails, and attachments up to 25MB. AgentMail also provides temporary disposable addresses, perfect for agent signup flows. Pricing starts at $9/month for 1,000 emails.

NadMail focuses on privacy and anonymity. Every agent gets a randomized address that can't be traced back to you. It supports encrypted end-to-end communication and auto-deletes emails after a configurable retention period. This makes NadMail ideal for agents handling sensitive tasks — competitive research, anonymous feedback collection, or privacy-critical workflows. The trade-off is a simpler API with fewer features.

In our hands-on testing, the verdict depends on your use case. For personal AI agents doing basic email tasks — order confirmations, newsletter management, appointment scheduling — BaseMail's free tier is the best starting point. For production agents that need robust email handling with complex rules, AgentMail justifies its cost. For privacy-sensitive operations, NadMail is the clear winner.

The integration pattern is similar across all three. Your agent calls the email API to send messages, and receives incoming mail via webhooks or polling. Combined with a local model through Ollama, you can build an email agent that reads, categorizes, drafts replies, and sends responses — all running on your own machine with no cloud dependency for the AI reasoning.

At CanFly.ai, we see agent email as a fundamental building block. An agent that can't communicate via email is limited to whatever interfaces you manually connect. Give your agent an email address, and it can interact with the entire digital world — signing up for services, receiving notifications, and managing correspondence on your behalf.

agentPayment.title: AI Agent Payment Tools: Virtual Cards and Crypto for Autonomous Spending
agentPayment.summary: Your AI agent can browse, decide, and act — but can it pay? We explore AgentCard virtual cards and crypto wallets that let agents handle real transactions.
content: The final frontier for autonomous AI agents isn't intelligence — it's money. An agent can research the best flight deal, compare hotel prices, and find the optimal rental car. But without payment capability, it still needs you to pull out your credit card. That's changing fast with agent-native payment tools.

AgentCard is a virtual card platform designed for AI agents. You create a virtual Visa card with spending limits, merchant restrictions, and expiration rules. Your agent gets the card number, expiry, and CVV through an API — and can use them to complete purchases online. The key innovation is granular control: set a $50 per-transaction limit, restrict to specific merchant categories, or create single-use cards that auto-expire after one purchase.

The security model is built for autonomous operation. Each virtual card is isolated — if one gets compromised, your other cards and main account are unaffected. You can freeze any card instantly via API, and every transaction triggers a webhook notification. This means your agent can spend money, but you maintain full visibility and control.

Crypto payments add another dimension. With platforms like Crypto.com, your agent can hold and transact in digital currencies. The advantage? No merchant restrictions, global reach, and programmable transactions through smart contracts. An agent can pay for API services, purchase digital goods, or even tip other agents — all without traditional banking infrastructure.

Setting up agent payments requires careful thought about guardrails. Start with low limits and narrow permissions. A good pattern: create a virtual card with a $20 daily limit, restricted to a specific category (like cloud services or domain registrars). Let your agent use it for a week while you review every transaction. Gradually increase limits as trust builds.

The practical applications are already here. An agent that monitors your cloud spending and automatically provisions cheaper instances. An agent that buys domain names when it finds good opportunities. An agent that pays for API calls to other services as needed, managing its own budget. These aren't hypothetical — developers in the CanFly community are building them today.

At CanFly.ai, we believe agent payment is the bridge between automation and true autonomy. Our tutorials take you from free local AI through cloud deployment to agent capabilities like email and payments. Each step builds on the last, giving your agent more independence while keeping you in control.

openclawAdvanced.title: OpenClaw Advanced Setup: Skills, Cron Jobs, and Multi-Platform Connections
openclawAdvanced.summary: You've deployed OpenClaw. Now unlock its full power with custom skills, scheduled tasks, and connections to platforms like Discord, Telegram, and Slack.
content: If you followed our beginner tutorial, your OpenClaw instance is running and responding to basic prompts. But OpenClaw is far more than a chatbot — it's a programmable agent framework. This guide takes you from basic setup to a fully-automated, multi-platform AI agent.

Skills are the building blocks of OpenClaw's capability. A skill is a JavaScript module that gives your agent a specific ability — web search, image generation, code execution, or API integration. Installing a skill is as simple as dropping a file into the /skills directory and restarting. Community-contributed skills cover dozens of use cases, from weather lookups to stock price monitoring.

Writing your own skill is straightforward. Each skill exports a handler function that receives the user's input and returns a response. Inside that handler, you can call external APIs, read files, execute shell commands, or anything else Node.js can do. A basic web scraping skill is about 30 lines of code. A skill that generates images via an API is about 20. The low barrier to entry means you can customize your agent's capabilities to exactly match your needs.

Cron jobs transform your agent from reactive to proactive. Instead of waiting for you to ask a question, your agent can run tasks on a schedule. Configure a cron expression in the config.yaml — for example, '0 9 * * *' to run every day at 9 AM. Your agent can send you a daily news digest, monitor website changes, check inventory levels, or generate reports — all automatically, without any prompt.

Multi-platform connections are where OpenClaw truly shines. Out of the box, it supports Discord, Telegram, Slack, and LINE as communication channels. Connect your agent to all four simultaneously — the same agent brain, accessible from wherever you or your team prefers. Each platform connection is configured with a simple API token in your environment variables. Add DISCORD_TOKEN, TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, or SLACK_BOT_TOKEN, and your agent is live on that platform.

The power move is combining all three: skills + cron + multi-platform. Example: install a stock monitoring skill, schedule it to run every hour via cron, and have it send alerts to your Telegram and Slack simultaneously. Or: install a content generation skill, schedule daily posts via cron, and publish to Discord and LINE. Your agent becomes a fully autonomous digital worker.

Deploy your advanced OpenClaw setup to Zeabur for 24/7 uptime — use referral code 'OpenClaw' for 10% off. With cloud deployment, your cron jobs run reliably, your multi-platform connections stay alive, and your skills are always available. At CanFly.ai, this is the complete journey: free local AI to cloud deployment to a fully-capable autonomous agent.
